title: Fifty connections against the flood
tagline: Reads doubled and 90% of queries are dying — the connection backlog is burying a 50-connection pool at 400 connections a second.
brief: You run an order-status service: an edge LB fronts an API tier that reads order rows from Postgres. At 09:00 a fulfillment partner switched on order-status polling — every active order, polled continuously. Traffic stepped from 1 200 to 2 400 reads/s. The API tier barely noticed. Postgres went from quiet to 90% errors in about a second.
symptom: Watch Postgres's node card the second the surge lands at t=10s: the backlog blows through the 50-connection pool instantly, the log fires 'using 800% of connection pool' and 'Connection pool exhaustion. 90% of queries failing', and p99 explodes past 15 seconds. Within a tick or two the node goes dark — the card freezes at 90% errors and 100% CPU. Arrivals only doubled from a 60% baseline.

phases.description: Partner polling goes live — reads double to 2 400/s and stay there

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Read-mostly order-status store; writes land via a separate low-volume ingest path.

diagnosis.question: Within a second of the surge, Postgres's error rate jumps from 0% to ~90% and the log prints 'Connection pool exhaustion' — yet arrivals only doubled. What's the root cause?

options.text: Arrivals (2 400/s) now exceed Postgres's ~2 000/s service rate, and every query that can't be served immediately camps on a connection — the backlog grows ~400 connections each second and buries the 50-connection pool almost instantly.
options.explain: Right. Little's Law: in-flight queries = arrival rate × time in system, and once service saturates, time-in-system — and with it the connection backlog — grows without bound, at λ−μ ≈ 400 connections/s here. The pool's drop rate scales with how far that backlog overshoots the pool, which is why a 20% capacity gap surfaces as ~90% of queries failing within the first second.

options.text: A 90% failure rate means demand is roughly 10× read capacity — readThroughputRps is drastically undersized. Raise it toward 20 000.
options.explain: The arithmetic disagrees: traffic doubled from a 60%-utilization baseline, so Postgres sits at ~120% of capacity, not 1 000%. Pure read-side saturation at 126% sheds about 13% of queries — the log instead prints 'Connection pool exhaustion' at 90%, which is the backlog of waiting queries overrunning a 50-connection pool, not a 10× throughput shortfall.

options.text: The API tier is saturated and queueing requests upstream — add Order API instances and the database errors clear.
options.explain: The Order API's card shows ~60% CPU and a 0% error rate: 16 instances at 4 ms/request is 4 000 rps of capacity against 2 400 arriving. The failures are on Postgres's card, downstream of a healthy tier — more API instances would only deliver the same doomed queries faster.

options.text: Client retries are amplifying the surge — each failed read is retried, multiplying load on Postgres until it collapses.
options.explain: No wire in this design carries a retryPolicy, and Postgres's inbound rate matches what the LB receives (2 400/s) — there is no amplification anywhere in the run. Retry storms are a real way to turn 'slightly over capacity' into 'dead', but the pool exhaustion here lands in the very first second of the surge, before any retry loop could even form.

fix.objective: Survive the partner launch at 2 400 reads/s: restore service-rate headroom and size the connection pool for peak concurrency, so Postgres stops shedding queries — errors back under 10% and p99 under 400 ms for the whole run.
hints: Little's Law: in-flight queries = arrival rate × time in system. Postgres serves ~2 000/s (1 900 read + 100 write) against 2 400/s arriving — the 400/s excess parks on connections, and activeConnections blows through the 50-connection pool inside the first second of the surge.

A bigger pool alone only buys seconds: while arrivals exceed the service rate the backlog keeps growing. Get the drain above 2 400/s first — readReplicas on Postgres's card multiplies read throughput (1 900 → 3 800 with one replica).

Click Postgres: set readReplicas to 1 and connectionPoolSize to 500. Capacity becomes 3 900/s against 2 400/s arriving (~62% utilization), and the pool now covers peak in-flight queries (2 400/s × ~0.2 s under load ≈ 480) instead of dropping them.
